title: Probing Quantum Information Scrambling via Local Randomized Measurements
abstract: In quantum many-body dynamics, locally encoded information typically scrambles across the entire system, becoming inaccessible to local probes. The upper bound of accessible information of local probes can be characterized by the Holevo information via optimal measurement. In this work, we investigate the information dynamics of quantum scrambling utilizing local randomized probes, quantified by the averaged accessible information (AAI). We derive an analytical expression for the AAI under Haar-random measurements and demonstrate that it is a function of purity of local reduced density matrix. Operationally, we employ the classical shadow protocol, using only single-qubit randomized Pauli measurements, to efficiently extract the AAI across extended subsystems. Through numerical simulations across diverse many-body paradigms, we show that the AAI can reveal distinct scrambling behaviors, resolving phenomena that range from dynamical confinement and ballistic transport to persistent scar revivals and many-body localization. This work highlights a pragmatic paradigm shift--from relying on optimal measurements to utilizing randomized local probes--for the characterization of complex quantum information dynamics.
summary: This paper introduces a new metric, Averaged Accessible Information, which can be efficiently extracted using randomized local measurements via the classical shadow protocol. It demonstrates that this metric can successfully distinguish between various many-body phenomena such as chaos, ballistic transport, quantum scars, and localization. This offers a pragmatic shift from needing optimal measurements to using simple, 'blind' randomized probes for studying quantum dynamics.
main_problem: Developing an experimentally tractable method to probe quantum information scrambling using local randomized measurements, overcoming the difficulty of implementing optimal state-dependent measurements.
main_result: The authors derive the Averaged Accessible Information (AAI), a metric that depends solely on local purity, and prove it is a robust, strictly concave diagnostic for various many-body dynamical regimes.
method: The framework utilizes the classical shadow protocol with single-qubit randomized Pauli measurements and employs Haar-random measurement theory and complex analysis to derive analytical expressions.
model_or_system: The framework is validated across diverse many-body paradigms, including the Mixed-field Ising model (chaos), Transverse-field Ising model (ballistic transport), PXP model (quantum scars), and Many-body localization (MBL).
key_observables: Averaged Accessible Information (AAI, denoted as chi_2 or Q2), Holevo information, Subentropy, and local purity.
important_parameters: Local subsystem size (LA), number of random unitaries (N), and disorder strength (W) for MBL.
main_assumptions: Assumes Haar-random measurements and utilizes the annealed approximation (averaging the log is approximately the log of the average), which is valid in high-dimensional Hilbert spaces.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the numerical convergence of the Q2 metric with increasing sample size; Figure 2 provides a spatiotemporal comparison of different information metrics (chi, chi_Q, chi_2) across the four studied models.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of scrambling measurement, derives the analytical form of the AAI metric, proves its mathematical properties like concavity, demonstrates its extraction via classical shadows, and validates its utility across various many-body models.
why_interesting: It provides a practical bridge between abstract information-theoretic bounds and experimental reality by showing that simple randomized local probes can capture complex, non-local scrambling dynamics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Convergence of AAI (χ2) via random Clifford sampling in the PXP model. Exact analytical evolution (solid black lines) is com- pared with statistical estimations for subsystem sizes (a) LA = 1 and (b) LA = 2. Estimations are obtained by sampling N ∈ {10, 50, 200} random unitaries. For LA = 1, samples are drawn uniformly from the 24-element Clifford group; for LA = 2, samples are generated via 50-depth random Clifford circuits. Colored lines denote mean values, with error bars indicating the standard deviation across 5 independent trials.

FIG. 2. Spatiotemporal resolution of information scrambling, χ2 versus Holevo χ. For each spatial site x and time t, the plotted color intensity represents the maximum value of the respective information metric evaluated over all subsystems of size LA = 2 that include the site x. The top row (a)-(d) displays the standard Holevo information χ, the middle row (e)-(h) displays the information from subentropy χQ, while the bottom row (i)-(l) shows our averaged accessible information, χ2. (a), (e),(i) confinement in MFIM; (b), (f), (j) ballistic coherent transport in TFIM; (c), (g), (k) persistent scar revivals in PXP; and (d), (h), (l) strict localization in MBL.

FIG. 3. Validation of the classical shadow protocol in non-ergodic regimes. The row (a)-(d) compare the exact evolution of the maximal χ2 (solid lines) with estimations from the classical shadow protocol (dotted lines with markers, Nshots = 3000) for various subsystem sizes LA ∈{1, 2, 3}. At each time step, the plotted maximum is evaluated over all � L LA  possible spatial combinations of the LA-site subsystem within the L = 10 chain. Each column corresponds to a distinct dynamical paradigm: (a) the PXP model exhibiting quantum many-body scars; (b) the many-body localized system; (c) the chaotic mixed-field Ising model; and (d) the integrable transverse-field Ising model.

FIG. 4. Dynamical confinement of information in the MBL phase. The solid green curve represents the dynamics within the full HMBL Hamiltonian system (L = 10), while the dashed red curve corre- sponds to an artificially isolated three-site cage (L = 3) sharing the same local disorder. The striking overlap of the high-amplitude os- cillations at early-to-medium times confirms that the effective l-bits are strictly localized, rendering the local dynamics oblivious to the extended thermodynamic bath.
